content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 24, 1991


Robert E. Leidigh
OLSON, CONNELLY, HAGEL,
FONG & LEIDIGH
300 Capitol Mall, Suite 350
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-91-189

Dear Mr. Leidigh:

This is in response to your request for advice regarding the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

QUESTIONS

1. Would reimbursement to your law firm for travel,
lodgings and meals in connection with speaking at a conference of
governmental relations officers of private companies be a
prohibited honorarium?

2. Should any permitted reimbursement to your law firm be
reported by you in your Statement of Economic Interests?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Reimbursement to your law firm for travel, lodgings and
meals in connection with a speech you give as a part of your
employment by your law firm is income to your firm, and is not a
prohibited honorarium.

2. Since the reimbursement is income to your firm, and your
ownership interest in the firm is less than 10 percent, you need
not report the reimbursement on your Statement of Economic
Interests.

___________________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-91-189
Page 2


FACTS

As a consultant to the Rules Committee of the California
State Senate, you are a designated employee of the Senate, and are
required to disclose your economic interests pursuant to Article 3
of Chapter 7 of the Act. (Sections 87300-87313.)

Additionally, you are a partner with an ownership interest of
less than ten per cent in the law firm of Olson, Connelly, Hagel,
Fong & Leidigh; your firm specializes in political law.

In February 1991, the managing partner of your firm received
a request to send a member of the firm to speak at the Annual
Conference of the State Government Affairs Council ("SGAC") to be
held in Austin, Texas from March 18-20, 1991. At the request of
the managing partner, you agreed to speak at the SGAC Conference.
SGAC had offered to pay for airfare and lodging costs for the
attendee from your law firm.

The topic of your presentation to SGAC was the new ethics
legislation in California. Your firm was selected to send a
representative to speak at the conference because of its
specialization in political law.

As of the date of your letter, your law firm had paid for all
costs in connection with the SGAC conference, with the exception
of car rental, beverages, meals in transit, and airport parking,
all of which were paid for by you. SGAC has asked that you submit
a reimbursement request for travel and lodging costs (including
meals). You are concerned that all or part of the reimbursement
might be considered to be a gift or honorarium to you.

ANALYSIS

An honorarium is defined in Section 89502(b) as "any payment
made in consideration for any speech given, article published, or
attendance at any public or private conference, convention,
meeting, social event, meal, or like gathering." However, a
payment to your law firm for travel and accommodations and meals
under the facts provided would not be an honorarium. Even though
you spoke at the SGAC conference, you did so as part of your
employment by your firm, and any payment made to the firm in
relation to that speech would be deemed to be income to the firm.

Income is defined in Section 82030(a) of the Act as follows:

(a) "Income" means, except as provided in
subdivision (b), a payment received, including but
not limited to any salary, wage, advance, dividend,
interest, rent, proceeds from any sale, gift,
including any gift of food or beverage, loan,
forgiveness or payment of indebtedness received by
the filer, reimbursement for expenses, per diem, or

Our File No. A-91-189
Page 3

contribution to an insurance or pension program
paid by any person other than an employer, and
including any community property interest in the
income of a spouse. Income also includes an
outstanding loan. Income of an individual also
includes a pro rata share of any income of any
business entity or trust in which the individual or
spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially,
a 10-percent interest or greater. "Income," other
than a gift, does not include income received from
any source outside the jurisdiction and not doing
business within the jurisdiction, not planning to
do business within the jurisdiction, or not having
done business within the jurisdiction during the
two years prior to the time any statement or other
action is required under this title.

As a partner with less than ten percent ownership in your
lawfirm, there would be no share of the payment from SGAC that
would inure to you under the Act. (Section 82030(a).) If your
interest were greater than ten percent, sources of income to the
firm would be sources of income to you, and you would be deemed to
receive a pro rata share of the firm's receipts. Since your
ownership percentage does not meet the threshold amount cited in
Section 82030, there would be no payment to you allocated from SGAC
reimbursement payment to your firm. (See Cadei Advice
Letter, A-86-246, copy enclosed.)

If you have questions regarding the above, please do not
hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

SUSAN L. BOBROW
Legal Counsel

SH:SLB:ken

Enclosure

2. Should any permitted reimbursement to your law firm be reported by you in your Statement of Economic Interests?

2. Since the reimbursement is income to your firm, and your ownership interest in the firm is less than 10 percent, you need not report the reimbursement on your Statement of Economic Interests.

, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

The topic of your presentation to SGAC was the new ethics legislation in California. Your firm was selected to send a representative to speak at the conference because of its specialization in political law.

sections.analysis:
An honorarium is defined in Section 89502(b) as "any payment made in consideration for any speech given, article published, or attendance at any public or private conference, convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like gathering." However, a payment to your law firm for travel and accommodations and meals under the facts provided would not be an honorarium. Even though you spoke at the SGAC conference, you did so as part of your employment by your firm, and any payment made to the firm in relation to that speech would be deemed to be income to the firm.

Income is defined in Section 82030(a) of the Act as follows:

(a) "Income" means, except as provided in subdivision (b), a payment received, including but not limited to any salary, wage, advance, dividend, interest, rent, proceeds from any sale, gift, including any gift of food or beverage, loan, forgiveness or payment of indebtedness received by the filer, reimbursement for expenses, per diem, or

contribution to an insurance or pension program paid by any person other than an employer, and including any community property interest in the income of a spouse. Income also includes an outstanding loan. Income of an individual also includes a pro rata share of any income of any business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater. "Income," other than a gift, does not include income received from any source outside the jurisdiction and not doing business within the jurisdiction, not planning to do business within the jurisdiction, or not having done business within the jurisdiction during the two years prior to the time any statement or other action is required under this title.

As a partner with less than ten percent ownership in your lawfirm, there would be no share of the payment from SGAC that would inure to you under the Act. (Section 82030(a).) If your interest were greater than ten percent, sources of income to the firm would be sources of income to you, and you would be deemed to receive a pro rata share of the firm's receipts. Since your ownership percentage does not meet the threshold amount cited in Section 82030, there would be no payment to you alloted from the SGAC reimbursement payment to your firm. (See Cadei Advice Letter, A-86-246, copy enclosed.)

2. Should any permitted reimbursement to your law firm be reported by you in your Statement of Economic Interests?

CONCLUSION: 1. Reimbursement to your law firm for travel, lodgings and meals in connection with a speech you give as a part of your employment by your law firm is income to your firm, and is not a prohibited honorarium.

2. Since the reimbursement is income to your firm, and your ownership interest in the firm is less than 10 percent, you need not report the reimbursement on your Statement of Economic Interests.

, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
